metadata.summary: Structured behavioral interviewing using STAR (Situation-Task-Action-Result) to force candidate specificity and comparability. Past behavior is the strongest predictor of job performance.

content.title: STAR Interview Framework
content.summary: The STAR framework (Situation-Task-Action-Result) is a structured behavioral interviewing technique that elicits four-part responses from candidates, forcing specificity and comparability across candidates. Without a response framework, candidates give generic, hypothetical, or team-credit answers that interviewers cannot score or compare. STAR follow-up probes force specificity; scored against a rubric, STAR responses become defensible hiring evidence.

why.p: Without a response framework, candidates give generic, hypothetical, or team-credit answers that interviewers cannot score or compare. STAR follow-up probes ("What was YOUR role, not the team's?", "What were the specific numbers?") force specificity. Scored against a rubric, STAR responses become defensible hiring evidence rather than gut impression.

list.item: Behavioral rounds in any structured interview process.
Evaluating leadership, communication, collaboration, adaptability, or problem-solving competencies.
Building a question bank for a new role or competency model.
Training interviewers who ask vague or hypothetical questions.
Technical skills assessment (live coding, system design) — STAR is not the right structure.
Screening for factual qualifications (certifications, tool experience) — a direct question is faster.
When the candidate has very limited work history (new grads) — combine with situational ("What would you do if...") questions.

section.name: star-components
section.p: A complete STAR response requires all four components; accept nothing as evidence until the Action and Result are specific and attributable to the candidate personally.

rule.statement: A complete STAR response requires all four components; accept nothing as evidence until the Action and Result are specific and attributable to the candidate personally.
rule.rationale: Candidates default to team-credit framing ("we delivered...") and omit results because they are uncertain what to share. Probing for personal contribution and quantified outcomes converts vague stories into scorable evidence.

section.name: component-definitions
section.p: Situation: the context or background — who, what, when, what was at stake. The interviewer prompt: "Tell me about a time when..."
Task: the candidate's specific responsibility in the situation — not the team's goal, the candidate's goal. Probe: "What was your role specifically?"
Action: what the candidate specifically did — concrete steps in first-person singular. Probe: "Walk me through exactly what YOU did, not what the team did."
Result: the outcome and its scope — quantified where possible, time-bounded, learning noted. Probe: "What was the outcome? What changed as a result? What would you do differently?"

rule.statement: Follow the four-step flow for every behavioral question: ask, listen for the Situation, probe for missing Task and Action specificity, confirm the Result.
rule.rationale: Skipping probes when the story sounds good is the primary source of false positives. Compelling narratives often lack the candidate's personal contribution — probing exposes whether the role was central or peripheral.

list.item: Step 1: Ask the behavioral question. Let the candidate answer without interruption.
Step 2: Listen for Situation. Note: is the context specific? Is there a timeframe? Who was involved?
Step 3: Probe for Task and Action. Ask: "What was your specific responsibility?" and "Walk me through exactly what you did." Distinguish personal action from team action.
Step 4: Confirm the Result. Ask: "What was the outcome?" and "What metrics can you share?" and "What did you learn?" If the result is vague, ask: "What would have happened if you hadn't done that?"

section.name: clarification-probes
section.p: "Can you be more specific about [the situation / the decision / the outcome]?"
"When did this happen? How long did it take?"

section.name: personal-contribution-probes
section.p: "What was YOUR contribution versus the team's?"
"If you hadn't been involved, what would have happened?"
"Who else was involved? What did they do specifically?"

section.name: quantification-probes
section.p: "What were the specific numbers or metrics?"
"How does that compare to before you made the change?"

section.name: learning-probes
section.p: "What would you do differently if you faced this again?"
"What did you learn from this experience?"

example.note: Complete STAR: "When the migration project fell 3 weeks behind (S), I was responsible for unblocking the backend team's dependency on the data model changes (T). I held daily standups with the data team, reprioritized 4 schema changes to unblock the critical path, and shipped a compatibility shim that let both teams work in parallel (A). We recovered 2 of the 3 weeks and launched within 1 week of the original date; the shim became a pattern two other teams adopted (R)."

Missing Action: "The project was falling behind, so our team fixed the dependency issues and we hit the deadline." No personal contribution visible; probe required: "What specifically did you do to fix the dependency issues?"

Missing Result: "I led a cross-functional team and we worked really hard on the migration." No outcome; probe required: "What was the result of the migration? Did you hit the deadline? What changed for the users?"

Hypothetical answer: "I would approach a situation like that by first aligning stakeholders, then setting up a project plan..." The question asked for a past example. Probe: "Can you give me a specific situation where you actually did this?"

rule.statement: Use leadership questions for any role where the candidate will influence, direct, or develop others; do not assume leadership only applies to management titles.
rule.rationale: Individual contributors lead without authority (technical decisions, driving standards, unblocking teammates). Leadership questions reveal influence mechanisms, not just direct-report management.

list.item: "Tell me about a time you led a team through a significant change."
"Describe a situation where you had to motivate an underperforming team member."
"Give an example of when you made an unpopular decision and how you handled the pushback."
"Tell me about a time you delegated an important task. What did you delegate and why?"

rule.statement: Prefer problem-solving questions where the candidate had incomplete information or time pressure; easy problems with clear solutions produce undifferentiated responses.
rule.rationale: Problem-solving under ambiguity is the most differentiating signal for IC and manager roles. Questions about "a complex problem" without constraints invite retrospective polishing; adding "with incomplete information" or "under pressure" reduces the ability to construct a generic answer.

list.item: "Describe a complex problem you solved at work."
"Tell me about a time you had to make an important decision with incomplete information."
"Give an example of when you identified a problem before others on the team noticed it."
"Describe a time you had to troubleshoot a critical issue under time pressure."
"Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult feedback or news to someone."
"Describe a situation where you had to persuade someone to change their position."
"Give an example of when you had to explain something complex to a non-technical audience."
"Tell me about a miscommunication that caused a problem. How did you resolve it?"

rule.statement: Include at least one conflict or disagreement question in every behavioral loop; candidates who only describe smooth collaboration have not been stress-tested on the most predictive dimension.
rule.rationale: How candidates describe conflict reveals their conflict resolution style, attribution tendencies (self-blame vs. other-blame), and ability to sustain relationships under stress — all strong predictors of team performance.

list.item: "Describe a successful cross-functional project you worked on. What made it succeed?"
"Tell me about a time you had a conflict with a colleague or stakeholder. How did you handle it?"
"Give an example of when you had to work with someone whose working style differed significantly from yours."
"Describe how you built a working relationship with a key stakeholder who was initially skeptical."
"Tell me about a time you had to adapt to a major change in your role, team, or company direction."
"Describe a situation where priorities suddenly shifted and you had to pivot your work."
"Give an example of when you had to learn something new quickly under pressure."
"Tell me about a time you failed at something important. What did you do, and what did you learn?"

template.purpose: Per-question STAR scoring grid with component scores and overall hire recommendation.
Before/during/after interview checklist for hiring managers using STAR.
